text: Total laparoscopic excision of type I choledochal cyst with row and y hepatic odigenostomy from University of Florida College of Medicine in Jacksonville.
Thirty-eight-year-old female patient presented with repeated episodes of upper abdominal pain. She was morbidly obese with multiple comorbidities.
ERCP was done and confirmed the diagnosis of choledochal cyst. The MRCP also was done and again the same diagnosis, type I choledochal cyst.
The video shows an outpouching at the upper part of the choledochal cyst, presenting a challenge during dissection. The lower common bile duct is visible within the intrapancreatic region.
Per distribution surgeon on the right side, assistance on the left side as you see in the picture.
During the diagnostic laparoscopy, the surgeon uses hooked diathermy to dissect the choledochal cyst from surrounding structures, carefully separating the cyst tissue.
The surgeon uses laparoscopic ultrasound to visualize the anatomy of the choledochal cyst, revealing thickened cyst walls and surrounding blood vessels, including the hepatic artery and portal vein.
The surgeon isolates and divides the cystic duct along with the accompanying artery. Subsequently, dissection of the medial part of the cyst from the portohepatic vessels is initiated.

The surgeon uses hook diathermy and a Maryland dissector to facilitate dissection, while laparoscopic ultrasound is employed to delineate anatomical relationships, aiding in the precise separation of the cyst from surrounding structures.
The surgeon meticulously performs further dissection in the area affected by chronic inflammation and scarring, using precise techniques to separate the tissue layers.
The surgeon dissects the cyst from the anterior wall of the portal vein, carefully exposing the entire portal vein in the lateral area.
The surgeon dissects the inferior part of the cyst from the duodenum and the head of the pancreas using a Merlin dissector and hook diathermy, carefully separating these structures.

The surgeon passes a Merlin dissector behind the caesantia to the portal vein, using a Penrose drain for handling. The intrapancreatic part of the common bile duct is dissected with hooked diathermy, revealing the thinnest section of the duct. An endo-GIA stapler is used to staple this part safely within the head of the pancreas.

After firing the stapler, the choledochal cyst and its posterior attachments are clearly visible and are dissected using a Maryland dissector and hook diathermy. The surgeon carefully dissects the medial aspect of the cyst and its attachment to the porta hepatis vessels.
The surgeon uses a suction tip for counter-traction and dissection in the surgical area, revealing the out-pouching of the choledochal cyst at its cephalad part.

The surgeon opens the choledochal cyst to identify the opening of the common hepatic duct. The cyst is completely separated and removed through an endobag. The wall of the cyst is separated from the common hepatic duct, and the surgeon inspects the opening to ensure the confluence is intact.

The surgeon proceeds to the second stage of the procedure by creating a Roux limb, measuring fifty centimeters distal to the duodenal-jejunal flexure. A jejunal transection is performed using a GIS stapler, followed by perpendicular division of the mesentery to ensure the Roux limb can reach the right upper quadrant without tension. The jejuno-jejunostomy is then created between the biliopancreatic limb and the Roux limb after firing the stapler.

The surgeon approximates and staples the common enterotomy, ensuring the lumen of the common channel remains unobstructed. An anti-obstruction stitch is placed, followed by closure of the mesentery in a running fashion.

The surgeon divides the transverse mesocolon to create a defect to the left of the middle colic vessels, allowing the end of the rulum to be pulled through towards the right upper quadrant. An enterotomy is created on the rulum, and the hepatico-jejunostomy is initiated using multiple interrupted sutures with absorbable suture material. The suturing begins at the angles, followed by the posterior wall, and concludes with the anterior wall.

The surgeon places an anchoring stitch between the periductal tissue and the anti-mesenteric surface of the rulum to reduce tension in the area. Subsequently, the defect in the transverse mesocolon is closed around the rulum to prevent potential herniation.
The surgeon completes the colocystectomy and places a drain in the right upper quadrant.

Post-operative course, operative time was two hundred fifty minutes. The patient was out of bed same day of the procedure. She was discharged post-op day two, followed in the clinic with good recovery.
Thank you.
